body:
in History, Music |   	April 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Above and below, you can watch┬Āmusi┬Łcians per┬Łform ŌĆ£Songs of Con┬Łso┬Łla┬Łtion,ŌĆØ a 1,000-year-old song set ŌĆ£to the poet┬Łic por┬Łtions of Roman philoso┬Łpher BoethiusŌĆÖ mag┬Łnum opus The Con┬Łso┬Łla┬Łtion of Phi┬Łlos┬Ło┬Łphy,ŌĆØ an influ┬Łen┬Łtial medieval text writ┬Łten dur┬Łing the 6th cen┬Łtu┬Łry. Accord┬Łing to Cam┬Łbridge Uni┬Łver┬Łsi┬Łty, the per┬Łfor┬Łmance of the piece, which had been lost in time until recent┬Łly,┬Ādid┬ŁnŌĆÖt come eas┬Łi┬Łly:
[T]he task of per┬Łform┬Łing such ancient works today is not as sim┬Łple as read┬Łing and play┬Łing the music in front of you. 1,000 years ago, music was writ┬Łten in a way that record┬Łed melod┬Łic out┬Łlines, but not ŌĆśnotesŌĆÖ as todayŌĆÖs musi┬Łcians would recog┬Łnise them; rely┬Łing on aur┬Łal tra┬Łdi┬Łtions and the mem┬Ło┬Łry of musi┬Łcians to keep them alive. Because these aur┬Łal tra┬Łdi┬Łtions died out in the 12th cen┬Łtu┬Łry, it has often been thought impos┬Łsi┬Łble to recon┬Łstruct ŌĆślostŌĆÖ music from this era ŌĆō pre┬Łcise┬Łly because the pitch┬Łes are unknown.
Now, after more than two decades of painstak┬Łing work on iden┬Łti┬Łfy┬Łing the tech┬Łniques used to set par┬Łtic┬Łu┬Łlar verse forms, research under┬Łtak┬Łen by Cam┬Łbridge UniversityŌĆÖs Dr Sam Bar┬Łrett has enabled him to recon┬Łstruct melodies from the redis┬Łcov┬Łered leaf of the┬Ā11th┬Ācen┬Łtu┬Łry ŌĆśCam┬Łbridge SongsŌĆÖ.
The song is per┬Łformed here┬Āby┬ĀBen┬Łjamin Bag┬Łby, Han┬Łna Mar┬Łti and Nor┬Łbert Rodenkirchen, three mem┬Łbers of the medieval music ensem┬Łble known as┬ĀSequen┬Łtia.


	

Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
If you would like to sup┬Łport the mis┬Łsion of Open Cul┬Łture, con┬Łsid┬Łer mak┬Łing┬Āa dona┬Łtion to our site. ItŌĆÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬Łtri┬Łbu┬Łtions will help us con┬Łtin┬Łue pro┬Łvid┬Łing the best free cul┬Łtur┬Łal and edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal mate┬Łri┬Łals to learn┬Łers every┬Łwhere. You can con┬Łtribute through Pay┬ŁPal, Patre┬Łon, and Ven┬Łmo (@openculture). Thanks!

What Ancient Greek Music Sound┬Łed Like: Lis┬Łten to a Recon┬Łstruc┬Łtion ThatŌĆÖs ŌĆ£100% Accu┬ŁrateŌĆØ
Hear the Old┬Łest Song in the World: A Sumer┬Łian Hymn Writ┬Łten 3,400 Years Ago

title: What Happens When a Globalized World Collapses: Archaeologist Eric Cline Explains How Bronze Age Civilizations  Adapted, Survived or Vanished
description:
in History |   	April 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

We live, as weŌĆÖre often told, in the era of glob┬Łal┬Łiza┬Łtion. In fact, weŌĆÖve been told i
body:
in History |   	April 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

We live, as weŌĆÖre often told, in the era of glob┬Łal┬Łiza┬Łtion. In fact, weŌĆÖve been told it so often over the past few decades that it now hard┬Łly seems like an obser┬Łva┬Łtion worth mak┬Łing. But how┬Łev┬Łer thor┬Łough┬Łly our era is defined by con┬Łnec┬Łtions between far-flung nations, soci┬Łeties, economies, and cul┬Łtures, we should┬ŁnŌĆÖt flat┬Łter our┬Łselves into think┬Łing we are pio┬Łneers in a whol┬Łly new glob┬Łal┬Łized real┬Łi┬Łty. As clas┬Łsi┬Łcist Eric Cline explains in this recent Big Think inter┬Łview,┬Āan inter┬Łcon┬Łnect┬Łed world flour┬Łished in the late Bronze Age, and espe┬Łcial┬Łly the four┬Łteenth and thir┬Łteenth cen┬Łturies BC. ŌĆ£Life was pret┬Łty goodŌĆØ in those days, he says, at least if you lived in one of the lands around the Mediter┬Łranean and Near East that con┬Łsti┬Łtut┬Łed what he calls the ŌĆ£ancient G8.ŌĆØ
The mem┬Łber peo┬Łples of this ret┬Łro┬Łspec┬Łtive orga┬Łni┬Łza┬Łtion includ┬Łed the Myce┬Łnaeans and Minoans in Greece, the Hit┬Łtites in mod┬Łern-day Turkey, the Assyr┬Łi┬Łans and the Baby┬Łlo┬Łni┬Łans in mod┬Łern-day Iraq, as well as the Cypri┬Łots, Egyp┬Łtians, and Canaan┬Łites. Alas, as implied by the title of ClineŌĆÖs 1177 BC: The Year Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion Col┬Łlapsed, their good times togeth┬Łer did┬ŁnŌĆÖt last.
In that book, and in lec┬Łtures on YouTube, heŌĆÖs explained the vari┬Łety of fac┬Łtors that con┬Łtributed to the dis┬Łso┬Łlu┬Łtion of that once pros┬Łper┬Łous ŌĆ£small-world net┬Łwork.ŌĆØ His sur┬Łpris┬Łing pop┬Łu┬Łlar┬Łi┬Łty for a his┬Łto┬Łri┬Łan of the Bronze Age owes in part to his will┬Łing┬Łness to draw com┬Łpar┬Łisons with that time and our own.┬ĀMany of his fans sure┬Łly found him out of curios┬Łi┬Łty over one ques┬Łtion: is our ŌĆ£flatŌĆØ twen┬Łty-first-cen┬Łtu┬Łry world sim┬Łi┬Łlar┬Łly head┬Łed for a col┬Łlapse?


	

If so, we might pay less atten┬Łtion to why the ancient G8 col┬Łlapsed, and more to what became of its for┬Łmer┬Łly inter┬Łde┬Łpen┬Łdent soci┬Łeties when the cri┬Łsis had run its course. Such is the sub┬Łject of ClineŌĆÖs After 1177 B.C.: The Sur┬Łvival of Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtions, and of the Big Think inter┬Łview extract at the top of the post. Some coped, some adapt┬Łed, some trans┬Łformed, and oth┬Łers sim┬Łply van┬Łished. Cypri┬Łots and the Phoeni┬Łcians of Canaan, for exam┬Łple, remade them┬Łselves to thrive in the chaos; the Egyp┬Łtians mud┬Łdled through with a mix┬Łture of adap┬Łta┬Łtion and cop┬Łing; the Myce┬Łnaeans and the Minoans lost more or less every┬Łthing, includ┬Łing their writ┬Łing sys┬Łtem, and had to rebuild from square one. But the tru┬Łly cau┬Łtion┬Łary tale is that of the Hit┬Łtites, whose civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion┬Łal anni┬Łhi┬Łla┬Łtion appears to have been in large part self-inflict┬Łed.┬ĀŌĆ£DonŌĆÖt be a Hit┬Łtite,ŌĆØ is one of ClineŌĆÖs pieces of advice; anoth┬Łer is to gain an under┬Łstand┬Łing of antifragili┬Łty┬Āsoon┬Łer rather than lat┬Łer.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
Why Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion Col┬Łlapsed in 1177 BC: Watch Clas┬Łsi┬Łcist Eric ClineŌĆÖs Lec┬Łture That Has Already Gar┬Łnered 7.6 Mil┬Łlion Views
Is Amer┬Łi┬Łca Declin┬Łing Like Ancient Rome?
G├Čbek┬Łli Tepe: The 12,000-Year-Old Ruins That Rewrite the Sto┬Łry of Civ┬Łi┬Łliza┬Łtion
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Contrast Reigns in Austn Fischer's Conspicuous Black-and-White Photos
description: ŌĆ£I started doing photography┬Āas a way to express things I donŌĆÖt understand or to convey a message IŌĆÖm having a hard time explaining,ŌĆØ Austn Fische
body:
ŌĆ£I started doing photography┬Āas a way to express things I donŌĆÖt understand or to convey a message IŌĆÖm having a hard time explaining,ŌĆØ Austn Fischer says. ŌĆ£I often work in quite a backwards┬Āway, knowing exactly what I want to arrange in front of the camera but struggling to understand the significance┬Āin my life until I am able to reflect on it after.ŌĆØ
The Wisconsin-born, London-based photographer taps into fashion as performance, considering how our garments, style, and gestures convey parts of our identities. Contrast is key in FischerŌĆÖs work, and it emerges through unusual pairings like lace ruffs atop athletic garb or an angular, black gown with a dainty, horse-shaped wire armature. Whether a portrait or a more conceptual composition, each work harnesses an exuberant sense of play and homes in on our ability to remake ourselves anew.
David Byrne
ŌĆ£Growing up, I had a lot of questions around my sexuality and my own experience as a man. I naturally gravitated towards fashion because of the story clothing can convey in an image,ŌĆØ he adds. ŌĆ£The colours, shape, fabric, and the way clothing wraps around a model create a unique conversation around identity and the body.ŌĆØ
Fischer has collaborated with a range of editorial and commercial clients, shooting Ai Weiwei with a milkshake for The New Statesman, for example, and David Byrne seemingly under oath for Crack Magazine. Whether working on a personal project or a commissioned series, the photographer transforms a largely black-and-white palette that could appear harsh into scenes exuding warmth and softness. 
Better understanding his own emotions is FischerŌĆÖs priority at the moment, following a series of painful experiences, both personal and professional, that have influenced what and how heŌĆÖs creating. ŌĆ£Recently, I spent two weeks from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m.┬Āpainting┬Āa wall in a church white over and over to understand┬Āpatience and reflect on myself. IŌĆÖm working a lot on understanding emotion and myself through putting my body through different tasks or challenges,ŌĆØ he tells Colossal.
Explore more of FischerŌĆÖs portfolio on his website and Instagram.

Ai Weiwei

David Byrne

schema.headline: Contrast Reigns in Austn Fischer's Conspicuous Black-and-White Photos

title: Water Samples from Around the World Melt into Dima Rebus' Dreamy Paintings
description: The act of painting is often seen as a solitary pursuit; we picture the artist alone in a studio, working through compositional puzzles and experi
body:
The act of painting is often seen as a solitary pursuit; we picture the artist alone in a studio, working through compositional puzzles and experimenting with materials of their own choosing. For Dima Rebus, the process is collaborative, although she may or may not know the other participants. In her large-scale works, the London-based artist adds new meaning to ŌĆ£watercolorŌĆØ as she incorporates water samples collected from strangers around the globe.
In her series Floaters, Rebus processes these crowdsourced units by freezing them with watercolor pigments, which she then allows to melt across the substrate, creating abstract color fields. She then adds figures and elements of landscape, often with a fluid, rippling effect evocative of light glinting off the surface of a lake, as aquatic themes emerge in the form of pools and swimmers.
ŌĆ£Noon FloatersŌĆØ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 140 x 300 centimeters
ŌĆ£Nearly every sample arrives with a letter, opening a dialogue shaped by place, mood, memory, and time,ŌĆØ Rebus says. ŌĆ£Over the years, IŌĆÖve built an archive of waters from rain, rivers, seas, oceans, and glaciers, each preserved as both material record and human message.ŌĆØ
Find more on the artistŌĆÖs Instagram.
Detail of ŌĆ£Noon FloatersŌĆØ
ŌĆ£Afterimage VIIŌĆØ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, and rainwater, 110 x 200 centimeters
ŌĆ£Nothing Matters Until An Empty Sofa Says Otherwise 1ŌĆØ (2026), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 110 x 86 centimeters
ŌĆ£Intuitive Course VIIŌĆØ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 42 x 80 centimeters
ŌĆ£Morning FloatersŌĆØ (2025), watercolor on paper, chemical solutions, rainwater, and water samples from strangers, 140 x 300 centimeters

title: Navid Baraty's Atmospheric Photos Explore Contrasting Scales of Time
description: When we consider that enormous metropolises like New York City and Chicago have only come into being within the past few hundred years, itŌĆÖs impos
body:
When we consider that enormous metropolises like New York City and Chicago have only come into being within the past few hundred years, itŌĆÖs impossible not to stand in awe of ancient cultural sites that have existed for millennia or geological features that expose millionsŌĆöeven billionsŌĆöof years of the planetŌĆÖs natural history. For Navid Baraty, the contrasts and tensions of contemporary urban life and timeless landscapes merge in otherworldly photographs.
BaratyŌĆÖs series The Time Between juxtaposes cityscapes with dramatic terrain, from desert dunes to snow-capped mountains. The project revolves around images in which two distinct digital photographs converge in a composite, drawing on the film technique of double exposure and exploring ideas of permanence, presence, and the ŌĆ£space between different scales of time,ŌĆØ the artist says.

The Time Between highlights iconic skylines like Manhattan and Chicago, pairing the outlines of skyscrapers and city lights with dramatic rock formations and atmospheric effects. ŌĆ£The process is intentional but restrained, combining the two frames through careful blending rather than constructing or artificially generating new elements,ŌĆØ Baraty tells Colossal. ŌĆ£IŌĆÖm interested in combinations that feel impossible at first, yet strangely convincing once they come together.ŌĆØ
See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Navid Baraty's Atmospheric Photos Explore Contrasting Scales of Time

title: An Interactive Archive Celebrates the Wide Ranging Projects Inviting 'Unruly Play'
description: ŌĆ£Play is how we give permission,ŌĆØ says Vitor Freire, co-founder of the Amsterdam-based studio Imagination of Things. ŌĆ£Permission to challenge what
body:
ŌĆ£Play is how we give permission,ŌĆØ says Vitor Freire, co-founder of the Amsterdam-based studio Imagination of Things. ŌĆ£Permission to challenge whatŌĆÖs fixed, rehearse what doesnŌĆÖt exist yet, and close the distance between people who wouldnŌĆÖt otherwise meet.ŌĆØ
Freire and co-founder Monique Grimord take play seriously and, in a new project, their studio created a vast repository of 169 artworks, designs, games, and more that have offered an unexpected encounter with imagination and joy. From Rael San FratelloŌĆÖs award-winning ŌĆ£Teeter-Totter WallŌĆØ to the healing Wind Phone project to a 12-foot puppet walking the world, Unruly Play is a multi-decade archive of participatory projects, public spaces, and digital creations that invite surprise and camaraderie.
ŌĆ£Our collaborators have always asked us where our ideas come from,ŌĆØ Gimrod says, ŌĆ£and the truth is that they come from references that rarely talk to each otherŌĆöit can be a seesaw through a border wall or a phone booth connected to the deadŌĆ” We wanted to create unusual dialogues and support new creative practices, and Unruly Play was our answer for that.ŌĆØ
Fully interactive, the project is searchable by theme or browsable through a shuffle feature. To dive deeper into the power of play, check out this compendium of artist-designed spaces.

schema.headline: An Interactive Archive Celebrates the Wide Ranging Projects Inviting 'Unruly Play'

title: Image of Family Torn by ICE Wins World Press Photo of the Year
description:
News
                ŌĆ£I hope it stirs people out of any sense of complacency,ŌĆØ said photojournalist Carol Guzy.
body:
News
                ŌĆ£I hope it stirs people out of any sense of complacency,ŌĆØ said photojournalist Carol Guzy. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            ŌĆ£Separated by ICEŌĆØ (2025) by Carol Guzy, ZUMA Press, iWitness, for Miami Herald (all photos courtesy World Press Photo)
    
        
            A heart-wrenching image of a tearful family torn apart by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has won the 2026 World Press Photo AwardŌĆÖs top prize.┬ĀThe contest, started in 1955, spotlights leading photojournalism and documentary photography. This yearŌĆÖs 42 global winners were chosen from a pool of 57,376 photographs by 3,747 photographers across 141 countries.The Photo of the Year, ŌĆ£Separated by ICEŌĆØ by American photojournalist Carol Guzy for the Miami Herald, was captured after an immigration court hearing in New York amid President TrumpŌĆÖs broader crackdown.ŌĆ£IŌĆÖve been following families to put a face on the consequences of government actions and rhetoric,ŌĆØ Guzy said in a video interview. ŌĆ£This photo should be painful to view, and I hope it stirs people out of any sense of complacency.ŌĆØFinalists for the award include Palestinian photographer Saber NuraldinŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Aid Emergency in GazaŌĆØ for the European Press Photo Agency (EPA) and American photojournalist Victor J. BlueŌĆÖs ŌĆ£The Trials of the Achi WomenŌĆØ for the New York Times Magazine.┬ĀPhoto of the Year Award finalist ŌĆ£The Trials of the Achi WomenŌĆØ (2025) by Victor J. Blue for The New York Times MagazineBlueŌĆÖs photograph, taken last May in Guatemala, shows Maya Achi women outside a courthouse after a 14-year legal process over wartime sexual violence during the countryŌĆÖs 36-year civil war. Of the 200,000 killed or missing during the war, 83% were Indigenous Maya.NuraldinŌĆÖs image, taken last July, shows Palestinians climbing onto an aid truck entering Gaza to get flour during IsraelŌĆÖs aid blockade. The UN reports that between late May and early October, at least 2,435 Palestinians were killed seeking food near aid sites.ŌĆ£My children are my first thought in this moment,ŌĆØ Nuraldin wrote on Instagram. ŌĆ£While I was documenting the hunger and suffering of people, they too were enduring the same hardship.ŌĆØPhoto of the Year Award finalist ŌĆ£Aid Emergency in GazaŌĆØ (2025) by Saber Nuraldin for EPA ImagesWorldwide winners in the Singles section also explore issues of displacement, justice, and war. Ukrainian photojournalist Evgeniy MaloletkaŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Russian Attack on Kyiv,ŌĆØ depicting 65-year-old Valeria Synium near her damaged home following a Russian missile strike, was one of three European winners.┬ĀA clear thread of grief runs through the awards, including in environmental crisis coverage. Tyrone SiuŌĆÖs ŌĆ£A Desperate PleaŌĆØ for Reuters, a winner in the Asia-Pacific and Oceania region in the Singles category, shows Mr. Wong crying as fire engulfs his house in Hong KongŌĆÖs Tai Po housing complex. His wife was among the 168 victims trapped in their homes.ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs a picture that tells you everything straight away,ŌĆØ Siu said in a Reuters article. ŌĆ£No matter where you are from in the world, you can feel what Mr. Wong is feeling, the helplessness and the pain.ŌĆØAsia-Pacific and Oceania (Singles), ŌĆ£A Desperate PleaŌĆØ (2025) by Tyrone Siu for ReutersBeyond single images, the contest also recognizes Stories and Long-Term Projects, both documentary forms that unfold over time.┬ĀThe images awarded in the 2026 World Press Photo Contest will be shown to millions worldwide as part of the organization's annual traveling exhibition across over 60 locations, debuting in Amsterdam at the De Nieuwe Kerk church tomorrow, April 24.

                        World Press Photo
                      
                      
                        Photography
                      
                      
                        Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)
